rescue_reasoning: Keep because climate relevance is explicit: the statement is titled “Climate Change in Macroeconomic Management and Public Finance” and aims to “mainstream climate change in macroeconomic management and public finance.” However, the deliverables are primarily enabling/management instruments—“toolkits and guides… green budgeting… green procurement and climate-informed public investment management” and “macroeconomic and fiscal assessments of… policies, including NDCs”—rather than direct mitigation actions/outcomes. Therefore the Mitigation primary category is too narrow; this is better captured as resource-allocation/financial-governance mainstreaming.
policy_statement: Implement the Santiago Action Plan 2020 workstream to mainstream climate change in macroeconomic management and public finance (Helsinki Principle 4), including tool development, guides for green budgeting/procurement/investment management, and assessments of policies including NDCs.
mechanism_description: Develops tools, toolkits, and guidance and supports assessments so climate risks and mitigation/adaptation considerations are integrated into forecasting, budgeting, procurement, and public investment decisions.
primary_causal_pathway: Public finance governance (budgeting/procurement/investment rules) → capital & purchasing shifted to low-carbon options → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Governments adopt climate-budgeting and climate-screening tools (e.g., green budget tagging, climate criteria in procurement, climate risk/abatement appraisal in public investment) and set their use in forecasts, budget preparation, and investment approvals.  
2) Mediating physical/economic process: Decision criteria and constraints alter the public project pipeline and purchasing patterns—favoring energy efficiency, clean energy, low-carbon transport/materials and discouraging fossil-intensive assets—thereby changing real-world energy use, fuel mix, and embodied emissions across supplied goods and infrastructure.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced direct and indirect (scope 1–3) GHG emissions from public operations and government-enabled infrastructure; secondarily, improved resilience by avoiding malinvestment in climate-exposed assets.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that climate-informed budgeting/procurement reallocates public spending and investment toward lower-GHG projects and away from high-emitting ones, reducing emissions economy-wide.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts through public finance rules and tools that reallocate spending and procurement toward low-carbon choices, yielding net ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While climate risk integration can materially improve adaptation outcomes (reduced exposure and losses), the broadest and most consistently climate-relevant effect across budgeting/procurement/investment mainstreaming is steering capital away from high-emitting assets and toward mitigation-aligned infrastructure and services.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific implementation focused exclusively on physical climate risk screening (with no emissions/low-carbon criteria), it could lean Adaptation; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes mitigation/adaptation considerations and “green budgeting/procurement/investment,” making mitigation the dominant common pathway.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that mainstreams climate considerations into public budgeting/procurement/investment inherently influences the emissions profile of public spending and assets; even when adaptation is emphasized, procurement/investment guidance typically includes mitigation-relevant carbon/energy criteria or avoids carbon lock-in.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy instance is more mitigation- or adaptation-heavy (e.g., resilience screening vs. carbon pricing in appraisal) requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some programs may be predominantly adaptation-focused if limited to climate risk/stress-testing of assets without explicit low-carbon budgeting/procurement criteria; conversely, if centered on green procurement and carbon accounting, mitigation dominance strengthens. Additionally, the mechanism is enabling/indirect, so realized impact depends on enforcement and uptake.
key_indicators: “Implement the Santiago Action Plan 2020 workstream”; “mainstream climate change in macroeconomic management and public finance”; “Developing tools”; “Preparing toolkits and guides”; “integrate climate in the policy and budget process”; “green budgeting”; “green procurement”; “climate-informed public investment management”; “Supporting macroeconomic and fiscal assessments… including NDCs”
co_benefits: Improved disaster risk management and resilience via “fiscal planning for climate change impacts” and “disaster risk management”; better adaptation/resilience policy appraisal alongside mitigation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information/tooling program (capacity building) rather than a planning strategy; however, the text emphasizes integrating climate into core public finance processes (“whole of government approaches” in budget/procurement/investment), so planning-strategy is the best fit.
